prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. They wrote this on a Sunday afternoon. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

- **Authentic voice**: The conversational, honest tone feels genuine and relatable. The writer doesn't try to make themselves look good, which is crucial for a failure narrative.

- **Clear structure**: The essay flows logically from setup → failure → aftermath → reflection, making it easy to follow.

- **Specific details**: Moments like "just stay out of the way" and not knowing what a pick and roll was make the story concrete and believable.

- **Meaningful reflection**: The insights are genuine, especially the conclusion about failure being "information" and "you don't know what you don't know." Multiple lessons are extracted naturally.

- **Good length management**: Hits the word count while covering the full arc of the experience.

## What Doesn't Work:

- **More showing, less telling**: The essay could use more sensory details and scene-setting. What did the gym smell like? How did it feel physically when realizing they were outmatched?

- **Rushed transitions**: The debate team pivot feels underdeveloped—this is interesting but gets only two sentences in the middle and deserves more attention.

- **Generic opening**: "The first time I really failed..." is a bit cliché. Could start with a more compelling hook.

- **Surface-level in places**: The emotional landscape could be deeper. The "I was really upset for a while" section tells us about feelings rather than showing them.

**Bottom line**: This is a solid, competent essay that fulfills the assignment. It's honest and well-structured with real insight, but it needs more vivid detail and emotional depth to be truly memorable.

**Late-loaded reflection** - Most insights come in the final two paragraphs. Weaving reflection throughout the narrative would create better balance.

My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. They wrote this on a Sunday afternoon. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7.5/10

## What Works:

**Strong narrative structure** - The essay has a clear arc from overconfidence to failure to growth. The chronology is easy to follow and keeps the reader engaged.

**Authentic voice** - This feels genuine and honest. The conversational tone makes it relatable, and your friend doesn't try to make themselves look better than they were. The vulnerability is a strength.

**Specific details** - "Pick and roll," the "stay out of the way" comment, Marcus making the team—these concrete moments make the story real and memorable.

**Meaningful reflection** - The lessons learned feel earned, not tacked on. The insight about "you don't know what you don't know" is particularly strong, and the connection to finding debate is a nice touch.

## What Needs Work:

**Too casual in places** - Phrases like "So yeah" and "Obviously" undermine the essay's credibility. Even personal narratives need some formality.

**Rushed transitions** - The jump to debate team happens in one sentence. This is a significant part of the "what you learned" section and deserves more development. How did you discover debate? What made it click?

**Telling over showing** - "I was really upset for a while" tells us the emotion but doesn't show it. What did that upset look like? 

**The ending feels list-like** - The final two paragraphs cover multiple lessons but read like separate points rather than a cohesive reflection. Try weaving these insights together more naturally.

**Bottom line:** This is solid work that clearly fulfills the assignment with honesty and self-awareness. With some polish and deeper emotional detail, it could be excellent.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Strong narrative structure** - The essay has a clear beginning, middle, and end that's easy to follow. The story flows logically from tryouts to failure to reflection.

**Honest and relatable** - The voice feels authentic. Details like "just stay out of the way" and the revelation that Marcus made the team create genuine moments that readers can connect with.

**Meaningful reflection** - Your friend extracts multiple legitimate lessons: casual vs. competitive competence, failure as redirection, and the "you don't know what you don't know" insight. The ending line about failure being "just information" is particularly strong.

**Addresses the prompt directly** - Stays focused on the assignment throughout.

## What Needs Work:

**Too much casual language** - Words like "pretty," "really," "totally," and "so yeah" make it feel like a rough draft. The writing needs more polish and precision.

**Telling over showing** - We're *told* "I was really upset" but don't really *feel* it. The emotional core could be stronger with more sensory details or scene-setting. What did that Friday feel like walking up to the list?

**Rushed in key moments** - The "stay out of the way" comment clearly left a mark, but it gets only surface treatment. The debate team pivot also feels abrupt—this needed a better transition or more explanation.

**Could go deeper** - The reflections are good but somewhat surface-level. Push further into why this mattered beyond the obvious lessons.

**Verdict:** Solid content, good bones, but needs another revision for sophistication and depth.
